title: Drive Design with Red-Green-Refactor - Quick Check

questions.question: What makes canonical TDD different from simply adding tests before coding?
questions.answer: Canonical TDD uses each test to expose the next behavior and steer design incrementally, not just to satisfy a timing rule about when tests are written.

questions.question: What is the best clue that your next TDD step is too large?
questions.answer: If writing the test forces you to invent several future abstractions or imagine half the system first, the step is too large.

questions.question: When does refactoring become necessary after a green test?
questions.answer: When the current code is readable enough to pass but is already making the next likely behavior harder to add or reason about.

questions.question: Which phrase best captures the course's view of TDD?
questions.explanation: The course consistently frames TDD as a behavior-design loop.

questions.question: What should usually drive refactor timing?

options.B: Whether design pressure makes the next step harder

questions.explanation: Refactor when the latest green step creates enough friction that the next step becomes riskier or less clear.
